perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise defined in this Article I, capitalized terms used in this Agreement shall have the meanings assigned to them in the Gathering Agreement. "Adjustment Claims" shall have the meaning set forth in Section 6.5.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise defined in this Article I, capitalized terms used in this Agreement shall have the meanings as commonly understood in the industry, irrespective of the Gathering Agreement. "Adjustment Claims" shall have no specific meaning unless explicitly defined in the context where it appears.
perturbation.explanation: The original text refers to the "Gathering Agreement" for definitions of capitalized terms. Modifying the text to state that terms have meanings 'as commonly understood in the industry' and ignoring the definitions in the 'Gathering Agreement' creates ambiguity. Furthermore, stating that 'Adjustment Claims' has no specific meaning unless defined in context introduces uncertainty and conflicts with standard contract interpretation practices, potentially invalidating the original definition within Section 6.5.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) § 1-303 - Course of Performance, Course of Dealing, and Usage of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts UCC § 1-303, which acknowledges course of dealing and usage of trade *after* the meaning of the agreements terms has been established. By prioritizing 'common industry understanding' over explicitly defined terms in a related agreement (the Gathering Agreement), the altered text disregards established contractual definitions and the importance of consistent interpretation, potentially violating commercial law principles. The UCC favors explicit agreements, where they exist.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-303. Course of Performance, Course of Dealing, and Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-303. Course of Performance, Course of Dealing, and Usage of Trade. (a) A "course of performance" is a sequence of conduct between the parties to a particular transaction that exists if: (1) the agreement of the parties with respect to the transaction involves repeated occasions for performance by a party ; and (2) the other party, with knowledge of the nature of the performance and opportunity for objection to it, accepts the performance or acquiesces in it without objection. (b) A "course of dealing" is a sequence of conduct concerning previous transactions between the parties to a particular transaction that is fairly to be regarded as establishing a common basis of understanding for interpreting their expressions and other conduct. (c) A "usage of trade" is any practice or method of dealing having such regularity of observance in a place, vocation, or trade as to justify an expectation that it will be observed with respect to the transaction in question. The existence and scope of such a usage must be proved as facts. If it is established that such a usage is embodied in a trade code or similar record , the interpretation of the record is a question of law. (d) A course of performance or course of dealing between the parties or usage of trade in the vocation or trade in which they are engaged or of which they are or should be aware is relevant in ascertaining the meaning of the parties' agreement , may give particular meaning to specific terms of the agreement, and may supplement or qualify the terms of the agreement. A usage of trade applicable in the place in which part of the performance under the agreement is to occur may be so utilized as to that part of the performance. (e) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (f), the express terms of an agreement and any applicable
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts UCC § 1-303 by prioritizing 'common industry understanding' over explicitly defined terms in the Gathering Agreement. Snippet 1 describes how course of dealing and usage of trade are relevant in ascertaining the meaning of agreements, but this is generally *after* express terms have been considered. The modified contract text directly undermines the value of express agreements by stating terms have meanings as commonly understood in the industry, irrespective of the Gathering Agreement, and even denying specific meaning to Adjustment Claims. This is a legal contradiction because it undermines defined contractual language, potentially violating commercial law principles that favor explicit agreements.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding any provision of this Agreement, in the event of an Emergency, Operator shall proceed with maintenance or repair work or with any other action when necessary to minimize damage and to end the Emergency ("Emergency Work"), without regard to the limits set forth in this Article IV. Operator shall, as soon as reasonably practicable, notify Owner of the existence or occurrence of the Emergency, setting forth the nature of the emergency, the corrective action taken or proposed to be taken, and the actual or estimated cost of such corrective action. Emergency Work shall include only such work as is necessary to immediately address the Emergency and does not include any work necessary to restore the Gathering System or improve the Gathering System in order to permit continued operations.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding any provision of this Agreement, in the event of an Inconvenience, Operator shall proceed with maintenance or repair work or with any other action when necessary to minimize the Inconvenience and to end the Inconvenience ("Emergency Work"), without regard to the limits set forth in this Article IV. Operator shall, as soon as reasonably practicable, notify Owner of the existence or occurrence of the Inconvenience, setting forth the nature of the Inconvenience, the corrective action taken or proposed to be taken, and the actual or estimated cost of such corrective action. Emergency Work shall include any and all work, at the Operator's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'Emergency' and limits 'Emergency Work' to actions needed to immediately address it. Changing 'Emergency' to 'Inconvenience' dramatically broadens the scope, as 'inconvenience' is subjective and less severe. Additionally, expanding 'Emergency Work' to include 'any and all work, at the Operator's discretion' removes the original limitations, potentially allowing unlimited and unnecessary work to be classified as 'Emergency Work,' thus creating legal uncertainty. This also removes the clause that the emergency work does not include any work necessary to restore or improve the gathering system.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 130.001 - Statute of Frauds
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing constraints on the scope of 'Emergency Work' and replacing the emergency definition with 'Inconvenience', the modification creates a scenario where the Operator could potentially authorize substantial expenditures without explicit Owner approval. This could contradict the Statute of Frauds, particularly under Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 130.001, if the cost of the 'Emergency Work' exceeds $25,000, because this change introduces ambiguity regarding the parties' obligations and could result in the enforcement of an oral agreement for work exceeding that amount.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ï»¿ CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE CHAPTER 130. LIABILITY PROVISIONS IN CERTAIN CONSTRUCTION CONTRACTS CIVIL PRACTICE AND REMEDIES CODE TITLE 6. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS CHAPTER 130. LIABILITY PROVISIONS IN CERTAIN CONSTRUCTION CONTRACTS The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see S.B. 687 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 130.001. DEFINITION. In this chapter "construction contract" means a contract or agreement made and entered into by an owner, contractor, subcontractor, registered architect, licensed engineer, or supplier concerning the design, construction, alteration, repair, or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenance, road, highway, bridge, dam, levee, or other improvement to or on real property, including moving, demolition, and excavation connected with the real property. Added by Acts 1987, 70th Leg., ch. 167, Sec. 3.14(a), eff. Sept. 1, 1987. Amended by Acts 2001, 77th Leg., ch. 351, Sec. 2, eff. Sept. 1, 2001. The following section was amended by the 89th Legislature. Pending publication of the current statutes, see S.B. 687 , 89th Legislature, Regular Session, for amendments affecting the following section. Sec. 130.002. COVENANT OR PROMISE VOID AND UNENFORCEABLE. (a) A covenant or promise in, in connection with, or collateral to a construction contract is void and unenforceable if the covenant or promise provides for a contractor who is to perform the work that is the subject of the construction contract to indemnify or hold harmless a registered architect, licensed engineer or an agent, servant, or employee of a registered architect or licensed engineer from liability for damage that: (1) is caused by or results from: (A) defects in plans, designs, or specifications prepared, approved, or used by the architect or engineer; or (B) negligence of the architect or engineer in the rendition or conduct of professional duties called for or arising out of the construction contract and the plans, designs, or specifications that are a part of the construction contract; and (2) arises from: (A) personal injury or death; (B) property injury; or (C) any other expense that arises from personal injury, death, or property injury. (b) A covenant or promise in, in connection with, or collateral to a construction contract other than a contract for a single family or multifamily residen
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a significant legal contradiction. By changing 'Emergency' to 'Inconvenience' and expanding 'Emergency Work' to 'any and all work, at the Operator's discretion,' the contract now allows for potentially unlimited and unnecessary work to be classified as 'Emergency Work.' This directly contradicts the implied intent, if not explicit wording, of typical construction contracts, as it could authorize substantial expenditures without proper owner approval, especially if the cost of such 'Emergency Work' exceeds $25,000. The Statute of Frauds, as potentially applied through Texas Civil Practice & Remedies Code § 130.001 (as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1 defining 'construction contract'), requires certain agreements to be in writing, particularly those involving significant financial commitments in construction contexts. The modification creates ambiguity regarding the parties' obligations and could lead to the enforcement of an oral agreement for work exceeding that amount, undermining the protections offered by the Statute of Frauds.

perturbation.original_text: Operator shall comply in all respects with all Environmental Laws and all Environmental Permits.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall comply with Environmental Laws and Environmental Permits only to the extent deemed commercially reasonable by Operator.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates full compliance with Environmental Laws and Permits. The modification introduces a 'commercial reasonableness' qualifier, allowing Operator to potentially disregard environmental regulations based on their own assessment of commercial viability. This contradicts the strict compliance typically required by environmental statutes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Clean Air Act, 42 U.S.C. § 7401 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the Clean Air Act (42 U.S.C. § 7401 et seq.), which requires strict adherence to emission standards and permitting requirements. The addition of 'commercially reasonable' allows the Operator to potentially bypass mandatory regulations based on cost considerations, thereby violating the Clean Air Act's intent and provisions for environmental protection. This is because the Clean Air Act is implemented via permits and other mandatory regulations - the Clean Air Act leaves no room for commercially reasonable non-compliance.

Clean Air Act Text Links to Regulations Find regulations: by topic by business sector General Information about EPA Regulatory Development Federal Register Notices are available at Federal Register Online EPA posts proposed and final rules at regulations.gov Air & Radiation Regulations and Statutes On this page: What is the Clean Air Act? Clean Air Act Table of Contents by Title What is the Clean Air Act? The Clean Air Act is the law that defines EPA's responsibilities for protecting and improving the nation's air quality and the stratospheric ozone layer. The last major change in the law, the Clean Air Act Amendments of 1990, was enacted by Congress in 1990. Legislation passed since then has made several minor changes. The Clean Air Act, like other laws enacted by Congress, was incorporated into the United States Code as Title 42, Chapter 85. The House of Representatives maintains a current version of the U.S. Code, which includes Clean Air Act changes enacted since 1990. This site provides links to sections of the U.S. Code containing the amended text of the Clean Air Act. Section numbers in the U.S. Code are different than the Clean Air Act's section numbers. The following table of contents gives corresponding section numbers in the Clean Air Act (CAA) and the U.S. Code (USC). Another difference is that titles in the Clean Air Act correspond to subchapters in the U.S. Code. Clean Air Act Table of Contents by Title Title I - Air Pollution Prevention and Control Part A - Air Quality and Emission Limitations (CAA § 101-131; USC § 7401-7431 ) Part B - Ozone Protection (replaced by Title VI) Part C - Prevention of Significant Deterioration of Air Quality (CAA § 160-169b; USC § 7470-7492) Part D - Plan Requirements for Nonattainment Areas (CAA § 171-193; USC § 7501-7515) Title II - Emission Standards for Moving Sources Part A - Motor Vehicle Emission and Fuel Standards (CAA § 201-219; USC § 7521-7554) Part B - Aircraft Emission Standards (CAA § 231-234; USC § 7571-7574) Part C - Clean Fuel Vehicles (CAA § 241-250; USC § 7581-7590) Title III - General (CAA § 301-328; USC § 7601-7627) Title IV - Noise Pollution (USC § 7641-7642). See Note at end of section. Title IV-A - Acid Deposition Control (CAA § 401-416; USC § 7651-7651o) See Note at end of section. Title V - Permits (CAA § 501-507; USC § 7661-7661f ) Title VI - Stratospheric Ozone Protection (CAA § 601-618; USC § 7671-7671q ) Note: The 1990 Clean Air Act Amendments added a new title IV, relating to acid deposition control, without repealing the existing title IV, relating to noise pollution. The U.S. Code designates the original title IV , noise pollution, as subchapter IV and the new title IV, acid deposition control, as subchapter IV-A. perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 'commercial reasonableness' qualifier, enabling the Operator to potentially sidestep mandatory environmental regulations based on their own commercial assessment. This directly opposes the Clean Air Act's requirement for strict adherence to emission standards and permitting requirements, as explained in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1 states that the Clean Air Act defines the EPA's responsibilities for protecting and improving the nation's air quality, implying that these responsibilities must be upheld and cannot be made subject to commercial considerations. The altered text weakens the original obligation of full compliance with Environmental Laws and Permits.
